analysis.period_summary: For the week of 2026-04-06 to 2026-04-12, the funnel generated $5,904.50 USD net revenue (ClickBank) across 113 frontend sales, with an average revenue per buyer of approximately $52.25. The single most critical finding is the checkout-to-purchase rate collapsing to sub-7% across all meaningful flows — with 1,635 combined checkout clicks producing only 105 CPV-tracked purchases — representing a severe and universal drop-off at the ClickBank checkout step that is suppressing revenue across every active variant.

funnel_scorecard.commentary: The funnel is healthy through opt-in and checkout click stages — landing-to-optin rates sit in the 14–20% range and offer-to-checkout rates are mostly strong — but all active flows share a critically low checkout-to-purchase conversion rate (5–7%), meaning the vast majority of motivated buyers who click the CTA are not completing payment. Backend monetization is average; OTO1 and OTO2 take rates are below benchmark and leave meaningful revenue on the table.

funnel_leaks.why_it_matters: With 1,635 checkout clicks across active flows this week, lifting checkout-to-purchase from ~6.5% to even 15% would approximately double frontend sales, adding an estimated 140+ additional purchases and over $5,000 in incremental frontend revenue at $37 AOV.

The /b/ landing page sends traffic to /offer/v2/ which converts 39% fewer visitors to checkout clicks than the v4 offer pages, meaning roughly 130 additional checkout entries per week are being forfeited relative to the stronger offer variants.

OTO1 (Blueprint $67) take rate sits at 29.2%, below the 40%+ strong benchmark — with 113 buyers, improving take rate to 40% would generate approximately 12 additional OTO1 sales worth ~$804 in incremental backend revenue per week.
OTO2 (Timeline $47) take rate of 16.8% is below the 25%+ strong benchmark; closing this gap to 25% across 113 buyers would add approximately 9 incremental OTO2 sales worth ~$423 weekly.

The /offer (non-versioned) page shows an anomalous 0.85% offer-to-checkout rate despite 352 offer page views, suggesting a broken or missing CTA link that is wasting any traffic reaching this page — sample size is small but the signal is unambiguous.

upsell_analysis.commentary: Backend monetization is underperforming relative to benchmarks: OTO1 take rate (29.2%) is in the average band but well short of the 40%+ strong threshold, and OTO2 (16.8%) is at the low end of average with the downsell almost completely unused (0.9% take rate). The OTO1 downsell at $47 is also poorly differentiated from the full Blueprint at $67, suggesting the pricing gap may not feel compelling enough to recover fence-sitters.

needle_movers.why: Every active flow shows checkout-to-purchase rates of 5–7%, far below the 20–30% target. With 1,635 checkout clicks this week, even a conservative improvement to 12% checkout completion would yield ~88 additional sales ($3,256 frontend revenue) plus backend multiplier.
what: Audit the ClickBank order form for friction: remove unnecessary fields, verify mobile rendering, check load speed and SSL trust signals
Add order bump or price-justification copy directly on the ClickBank checkout page (e.g. 'What you get for $37' summary)
Test a 3-day urgency or scarcity element on the sales page immediately before the CTA to pre-sell the checkout decision, reducing cold clicks
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Improving checkout-to-purchase from 6.5% to 12% across 1,635 weekly checkout clicks adds ~88 frontend sales ≈ $3,256 frontend + ~$1,500 estimated backend upsell revenue per week.

needle_movers.why: OTO1 Blueprint take rate is 29.2% against a 40%+ benchmark; with 113 buyers, closing to 40% means 12 additional Blueprint sales at $67 = ~$804/week in recoverable revenue with no additional traffic cost.
what: Rewrite the OTO1 page headline to lead with the specific outcome gap the Blueprint fills that the core product does not cover
Add a short (2–3 min) video or audio testimonial from a buyer who specifically purchased the Blueprint above the fold
A/B test reducing OTO1 price to $57 or adding a limited-quantity framing to improve impulse acceptance rate
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting OTO1 take rate from 29% to 40% generates approximately $804 additional revenue per week at current frontend sales volume, scaling proportionally if checkout fix is implemented simultaneously.

needle_movers.why: The /b/ → /offer/v2/ flow converts only 60.6% of offer viewers to checkout clicks versus 77–87% for v4-based offers; this gap costs approximately 82 checkout entries per week from this flow alone.
what: Audit /offer/v2/ CTA placement, button copy, and page length versus /offer/v4/ — identify structural differences causing the drop
Test replacing the /offer/v2/ CTA section with the v4 CTA block while keeping the rest of v2 copy intact
If /b/ landing page traffic is email-sourced, consider routing /b/ subscribers directly to /offer/v4/ to leverage the stronger offer page
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting /b/ offer-to-checkout from 60.6% to 80% adds ~66 checkout clicks/week; at current 5.34% purchase rate that is ~3–4 additional sales ($111–$148 frontend) plus upsell tail — directional uplift.

needle_movers.area: OTO2 take rate and downsell optimization
needle_movers.why: OTO2 Timeline take rate of 16.8% is below the 25% benchmark, and the downsell is nearly invisible at 0.9% — the downsell may have a redirect or presentation issue, and the OTO2 page itself may need urgency or value reinforcement.
what: Verify OTO2 downsell redirect is functioning correctly — 0.9% take rate on a $37 offer for people who declined $47 suggests most decliners are not seeing or engaging with the downsell page
Add a 'why you need this now' urgency block to the OTO2 page specific to the timing/timeline angle of the product
Test lowering OTO2 downsell to $27 to create a more compelling price contrast with the $47 full price
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting OTO2 take rate from 16.8% to 25% adds ~9 Timeline sales at $47 = ~$423/week; fixing the downsell funnel could recover an additional $27–$37 per declining buyer.

needle_movers.why: The /offer page shows 352 offer page views but only 3 checkout clicks (0.85% offer-to-checkout), which is anomalously broken relative to every other offer page and indicates a non-functional or missing CTA rather than a copy problem.
what: Immediately QA the /offer page CTA button link — confirm ClickBank hop URL is correctly embedded and not returning a 404 or redirecting incorrectly
Check whether this page is being served to live traffic intentionally (email campaign 92 with 351 views appears to route here) and pause or redirect if broken
Once CTA is fixed, assess whether /offer copy is competitive with v4 variants before scaling traffic back to it
needle_movers.revenue_impact: At a corrected 80% offer-to-checkout rate, 352 weekly viewers would generate ~282 checkout clicks; even at the current low 6.5% purchase rate that is ~18 additional sales worth ~$666/week — though this flow may be intentionally low-traffic.

cpv_cb_discrepancy.note: CPV Labs is under-counting purchases by 8 relative to ClickBank (105 vs 113 frontend sales), indicating missing ClickBank postback fires — likely from traffic that completed purchase outside tracked CPV sessions; postback configuration should be audited to ensure all ClickBank conversion events are firing back to CPV.

analysis.data_notes: 1) The 'landing→offer' flow shows a large discrepancy between landing_page_views (25) and offer_page_views (352), suggesting direct/untracked traffic is landing on /offer without passing through a CPV-tracked landing page — this inflates the offer_page_views count for that flow and makes the offer_to_checkout rate (0.85%) even more anomalous. 2) GA4 supplement data covers funnel_v4 and funnel_v2 aggregated funnels only and cannot be mapped 1:1 to individual CPV flow keys; optin_page_to_submit rates (38.85% for v4, 45.83% for v2) are above the 30% caution threshold and are healthy, suggesting the opt-in page itself is not a primary leak. 3) funnel_backend.variant_sales shows 84 of 113 sales attributed to 'other' with $2,688.88 revenue, meaning the majority of ClickBank sales cannot be attributed to a specific named funnel variant — ClickBank variant tracking parameters may need review. 4) CPV campaign 100 shows 12.5% CR on only 8 views and 1 conversion — statistically meaningless, flag as noise.
